name: IB Chemistry
questions: Where does the type of bonding in a substance directly explain one of its physical properties?
Where does a periodic trend down a group predict a change in chemical behavior of the elements?
Where do delocalized electrons give a chemical species unusual or distinctive properties?
Where does hydrogen bonding cause a substance to have a higher boiling point or unusual solubility than expected?
Which molecules are non-polar despite containing polar covalent bonds?
Where does the same element form allotropes with very different physical and chemical properties?
Where does the charge density of an ion directly determine the strength of an ionic bond or lattice?
Which spectroscopic technique gives direct evidence for a specific structural feature of a molecule?
Where does an atom's electron configuration directly predict its most common oxidation state?
Where does resonance lower the energy of a species compared with what a single Lewis structure predicts?
Where does a pi bond change both the geometry and the chemical reactivity of a molecule?
Which substances can conduct electricity in the solid state, and what structural feature makes this possible?
Where does increasing chain length in a homologous series produce a predictable trend in a physical property?
Which properties of transition elements arise specifically from their incomplete d-sublevels?
Where does a chiral carbon give rise to two molecules with the same formula but different properties?
Which structural isomers share a molecular formula but belong to different functional group classes?
Where does the degree of unsaturation in an organic molecule affect both its reactivity and its spectroscopic fingerprint?
Where does a change in temperature simultaneously shift both the rate and the equilibrium position of a reaction?
Which reactions release energy because the product bonds are collectively stronger than the reactant bonds?
Where does Le Chatelier's principle predict the direction a system at equilibrium will shift in response to a change?
Which chemical species can act as both a proton donor and a proton acceptor depending on context?
Where does the structure of an acid or base explain its relative strength?
Where does a catalyst increase the rate of a reaction without being consumed in the overall reaction?
Where does the strength of a bond directly influence the rate of a reaction that breaks that bond?
Which electrochemical systems convert chemical energy to electrical energy spontaneously?
Where does the mechanism of a multi-step reaction determine the form of the rate equation?
Which species act as nucleophiles in both inorganic and organic chemistry?
Which reactions produce a mixture of products because a reactive intermediate lacks selectivity?
Where does an increase in entropy drive a process that is not exothermic?
Which acid-base reactions are also well described using Lewis acid-base theory?
Where do standard electrode potential data predict whether a redox reaction will be spontaneous?
Which functional group transformations in organic chemistry involve a change in the oxidation state of carbon?
Where does the identity of the leaving group affect the rate of a substitution reaction?
Where does limiting the supply of one reactant change both the products formed and the health or environmental consequences?
Which reactions can be classified as both an acid-base reaction and a redox reaction simultaneously?

options.text: NaCl high melting point
options.why: Ionic bonding requires large energy to separate the strongly attracted opposing ions.

options.text: Copper electrical conductivity
options.why: Metallic bonding provides delocalized electrons that carry charge freely through the solid.

options.text: Iodine low melting point
options.why: Iodine melts easily because of weak dispersion forces between I₂ molecules, not the covalent bond inside them.

options.text: Silicon dioxide very high melting point
options.why: Covalent network bonding in SiO₂ requires breaking many strong Si-O bonds throughout the entire lattice.

options.text: Magnesium malleability
options.why: Metallic bonding lets cation layers slide without breaking the non-directional electron sea.

options.text: Ethanol dissolving freely in water
options.why: Ethanol mixes with water due to hydrogen bonding between molecules, not the covalent bonds within each molecule.

options.text: Alkali metal reactivity increasing down group 1
options.why: Falling ionization energy and greater shielding make each successive alkali metal react more vigorously with water.

options.text: Halogen oxidizing power decreasing down group 17
options.why: Each halogen is a weaker oxidizing agent than the one above it as electron affinity decreases down the group.

options.text: Atomic radius increasing down group 2
options.why: Atomic radius increases as electron shells are added, but this structural measurement does not by itself predict chemical behavior.

options.text: HX acid strength increasing from HF to HI
options.why: H-X bond strength decreases down group 17, making proton donation progressively easier from HF to HI.

options.text: Halide reducing power increasing from Cl- to I-
options.why: Outer electrons of iodide sit further from the nucleus and are more easily removed, making I- a stronger reducing agent than Cl-.

options.text: Electronegativity increasing from Li to F across period 2
options.why: Electronegativity rises across period 2, but this is a horizontal trend, not a change in behavior going down a group.

options.text: Iron metallic bonding and electrical conductivity
options.why: Delocalized electrons throughout the iron lattice move freely in any direction under an applied voltage.

options.text: Benzene resistance to electrophilic addition
options.why: Delocalization of pi electrons over the ring lowers the system energy and makes addition unfavorable relative to substitution.

options.text: Polar C-F bond in fluoromethane
options.why: Fluoromethane has a polar C-F bond from electronegativity difference, but bond polarity arises from localized electrons, not delocalization.

options.text: Carbonate ion equal C-O bond lengths
options.why: Delocalized pi electrons spread equally over all three C-O bonds, making them identical in length and strength.

options.text: Graphite conducting electricity unlike other solid non-metals
options.why: One delocalized pi electron per carbon extends through each layer, giving graphite exceptional non-metallic conductivity.

options.text: NaCl ions carrying charge in aqueous solution
options.why: Dissolved NaCl conducts because Na+ and Cl- ions are mobile, not because any electrons are delocalized.

options.text: Water anomalously high boiling point vs H₂S
options.why: Water boils at 100 C rather than the negative 80 C predicted by group 16 trends, because of strong hydrogen bonds between molecules.

options.text: Ammonia boiling above the group 15 trend
options.why: NH₃ boils at -33 C while PH₃ boils at -87 C; hydrogen bonding in ammonia elevates the boiling point significantly above the trend.

options.text: HCl slightly elevated boiling point vs HBr
options.why: HCl boils a little above the group 17 trend due to permanent dipole-dipole interactions, not hydrogen bonding.

options.text: DNA double helix held together without covalent bonds
options.why: Hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs hold the two strands together, stabilizing the double helix.

options.text: Water high specific heat capacity
options.why: Hydrogen bonds must continuously break and re-form as water is heated, requiring more energy per degree than most liquids.

options.text: Noble gas boiling points rising down group 18
options.why: Noble gas boiling points increase with growing London dispersion forces as electron clouds enlarge, not because of hydrogen bonding.

options.text: Carbon dioxide CO₂ linear molecule
options.why: Two C=O dipoles point in exactly opposite directions and cancel, giving a zero net dipole moment.

options.text: Tetrachloromethane CCl₄ tetrahedral molecule
options.why: Four identical C-Cl dipoles are symmetrically arranged and cancel completely, giving zero net dipole moment.

options.text: Water H₂O bent geometry
options.why: Water has two polar O-H bonds, but the bent geometry means the dipoles do not cancel, so water is polar.

options.text: Boron trifluoride BF₃ trigonal planar
options.why: Three B-F dipoles arranged symmetrically in a plane cancel completely, giving zero net dipole moment.

options.text: Sulfur hexafluoride SF₆ octahedral
options.why: Six identical S-F dipoles arranged octahedrally cancel in opposing pairs, producing a non-polar molecule.

options.text: Sodium chloride NaCl ionic compound
options.why: NaCl forms an ionic lattice rather than discrete molecules, so the concept of a molecular dipole moment does not apply.

options.text: Diamond vs graphite as carbon allotropes
options.why: Diamond is an insulating giant covalent lattice; graphite is a soft conductor with delocalized pi electrons. Both are pure carbon.

options.text: Dioxygen O₂ vs ozone O₃
options.why: O₂ is stable and essential for respiration while O₃ is reactive and toxic, reflecting different bonding and molecular geometry.

options.text: Ice vs liquid water
options.why: Ice and liquid water have different densities and properties, but both are H₂O in different states, not allotropes of an element.

options.text: White phosphorus vs red phosphorus
options.why: White P₄ is waxy, highly reactive and toxic; red phosphorus is a stable covalent network. Both are pure phosphorus.

options.text: Buckminsterfullerene C₆₀ vs graphene
options.why: C₆₀ is a molecular cage and graphene a planar single-atom layer; both are pure carbon with completely different properties.

options.text: Iron(II) oxide vs iron(III) oxide
options.why: FeO and Fe₂O₃ have different properties, but they are different compounds of iron at different oxidation states, not allotropes.

options.text: MgO much higher melting point than NaCl
options.why: Mg₂+ and O₂- have higher charge and smaller radius than Na+ and Cl-, giving MgO a far stronger lattice and higher melting point.

options.text: Li salts having larger lattice enthalpies than Cs salts
options.why: Li+ is smaller with greater charge density than Cs+, attracting anions more strongly and increasing lattice enthalpy.

options.text: Transition metal ions forming colored complexes
options.why: Colors in transition metal complexes arise from d-orbital splitting by ligand field, not from charge density differences in a lattice.

options.text: Al₃+ releasing more enthalpy on hydration than Na+
options.why: Al₃+ has very high charge density and pulls water dipoles in strongly, releasing a large negative hydration enthalpy.

options.text: Fe₃+ forming stronger lattice than Fe₂+ in iron oxides
options.why: Fe₃+ has higher charge and smaller radius than Fe₂+, giving iron(III) compounds stronger ionic lattices.

options.text: Group 1 fluoride vs iodide solubility trends
options.why: Solubility patterns in group 1 halides reflect a balance of lattice and hydration enthalpies, not charge density alone.

options.text: 1H NMR signal at 9-10 ppm chemical shift
options.why: Only an aldehyde CHO proton resonates this far downfield, providing direct evidence for an aldehyde group.

options.text: IR broad absorption at 2500-3300 cm-1
options.why: Broad O-H stretching in this region directly identifies a carboxylic acid, distinguishing it from other OH-containing groups.

options.text: Molecular ion peak m/z in mass spectrum
options.why: A molecular ion peak gives the relative molecular mass but alone provides no direct evidence for connectivity or functional groups.

options.text: IR carbonyl absorption at 1700-1750 cm-1
options.why: Strong absorption in this region is a direct fingerprint of C=O stretching, indicating a carbonyl functional group.

options.text: 1H NMR quartet adjacent to a triplet
options.why: A quartet next to a triplet is the coupling signature of a CH₃-CH₂ unit, directly revealing an ethyl group in the molecule.

options.text: Flame test orange-yellow color for sodium
options.why: Orange-yellow flame color identifies sodium by atomic emission, not a molecular technique for identifying structural features.

options.text: Sodium forming Na+ in all its compounds
options.why: Na has one 3s electron beyond the neon core; losing it achieves a noble gas configuration, directly predicting +1.

options.text: Chlorine forming Cl- in ionic compounds
options.why: Seven valence electrons means gaining one achieves an octet, directly predicting the -1 state from the configuration.

options.text: Iron forming both Fe₂+ and Fe₃+
options.why: Iron's two common states arise from similar successive ionization energies for 4s and 3d electrons, not from a single configuration prediction.

options.text: Zinc forming only Zn₂+ in its compounds
options.why: Zinc's 3d10 4s2 configuration gives a stable full d-shell; only the two 4s electrons are removed, predicting +2 exclusively.

options.text: Fluorine forming only F- in its compounds
options.why: Fluorine's 2p5 configuration invariably accepts one electron to reach the neon configuration, giving only -1.

options.text: Carbon forming CO rather than CO₂ in limited oxygen
options.why: Whether carbon produces CO or CO₂ depends on oxygen availability and thermodynamics, not on carbon's electron configuration.

options.text: Benzene lower heat of hydrogenation than three isolated double bonds
options.why: Benzene releases about 150 kJ/mol less on hydrogenation than three isolated double bonds would predict, quantifying resonance stabilization.

options.text: Carbonate ion CO₃ 2- more stable than any one Lewis structure
options.why: Delocalization spreads double-bond character equally over all three C-O bonds, giving lower energy than any fixed localized structure.

options.text: SO₂ bent molecular geometry
options.why: Sulfur dioxide is bent because a lone pair on sulfur creates electron repulsion as VSEPR predicts; the shape is not a result of resonance energy lowering.

options.text: Nitrate ion NO₃- equal bond lengths and symmetry
options.why: Resonance distributes pi electrons equally over all three N-O bonds, stabilizing NO₃- below the energy of any localized structure.

options.text: Carboxylate RCOO- more stable than alkoxide RO-
options.why: Resonance delocalizes the negative charge over both oxygens in RCOO-, lowering its energy compared with a localized charge on one oxygen in RO-.

options.text: Expanded octet in PCl₅ with ten bonding electrons
options.why: PCl₅ uses d-orbitals for five bonds, exceeding the octet, but this is orbital expansion, not resonance stabilization through delocalization.

options.text: Ethene C=C restricted rotation and electrophilic addition
options.why: Pi bond prevents rotation around C=C, enforces planar geometry at each carbon, and enables electrophilic addition reactions.

options.text: Carbonyl group C=O as nucleophilic addition site
options.why: Pi bond in C=O makes the carbonyl carbon planar and creates an electrophilic center that invites nucleophilic attack.

options.text: Lone pair on nitrogen in methylamine
options.why: Nitrogen's lone pair makes methylamine nucleophilic and basic, but it is not a pi bond and does not constrain molecular geometry.

options.text: Benzene ring planar with electrophilic substitution
options.why: Cyclic pi delocalization makes benzene planar and, through resonance stabilization, favors substitution over addition.

options.text: Nitrile C-N triple bond linear geometry and electrophilicity
options.why: Two pi bonds in C-triple bond-N give linear geometry and make the nitrile carbon an electrophilic site for nucleophilic addition.

options.text: Coordinate bond in hexaamminecobalt(III) ion
options.why: Ammonia lone pairs form coordinate sigma bonds to cobalt; no pi bonds are involved and geometry is set by d-orbital effects.

options.text: Iron rod conducting electricity as a solid
options.why: Delocalized electrons in the metallic lattice move freely in any direction under an applied potential difference.

options.text: Graphite pencil lead conducting electricity
options.why: One delocalized pi electron per carbon extends through each graphite layer, giving conductivity unlike most solid non-metals.

options.text: Solid sodium chloride non-conducting
options.why: NaCl contains charged ions, but they are held rigidly in the lattice and cannot move to carry charge in the solid state.

options.text: Copper wire conducting current throughout its length
options.why: Copper's metallic bonding provides a continuous sea of mobile delocalized electrons through the entire solid.

options.text: Tungsten filament conducting in a light bulb
options.why: Tungsten conducts as a solid metal because of delocalized 6s and 5d electrons in its metallic lattice.

options.text: Molten lead conducting electricity
options.why: Molten lead conducts because ions become mobile when the solid lattice breaks down; this is liquid-state conductivity, not solid-state.

options.text: Boiling points of straight-chain alkanes C₁ to C₁₀
options.why: Each additional CH₂ unit adds roughly 25 C to the boiling point due to stronger London dispersion forces along longer chains.

options.text: Melting points of saturated straight-chain fatty acids
options.why: Longer saturated fatty acids have higher melting points because of increased van der Waals contact area between the aligned chains.

options.text: Reactivity of alkanes toward electrophilic reagents
options.why: Alkanes are essentially unreactive toward electrophiles regardless of chain length; no predictable trend exists for this type of reactivity.

options.text: Viscosity of liquid alkanes from pentane to decane
options.why: Longer chains entangle more and interact more strongly via London forces, giving a predictable viscosity increase with chain length.

options.text: Solubility of primary alcohols in water from C₁ to C₅
options.why: Short-chain alcohols are miscible with water, but solubility falls predictably as the non-polar chain grows beyond the -OH group's influence.

options.text: Acid strength of straight-chain carboxylic acids C₂ to C₆
options.why: Adding CH₂ units barely changes pKa; the -COOH group is too distant from the chain extension to be influenced by its length.

options.text: Manganese showing oxidation states from +2 to +7
options.why: Mn's 3d5 4s2 configuration allows successive removal of d-electrons at similar energies, giving many accessible oxidation states.

options.text: Blue color of aqueous copper(II) sulfate
options.why: Cu₂+'s incomplete 3d sublevel is split by water ligands; d-d transitions absorb visible light and produce the characteristic blue color.

options.text: High density of iron compared with calcium
options.why: Iron is denser than calcium because of a smaller atomic radius and greater nuclear charge, not specifically because the d-sublevel is incomplete.

options.text: Iron catalyzing ammonia synthesis in the Haber process
options.why: Variable oxidation states from the incomplete d-sublevel allow iron to adsorb and release reactants during heterogeneous catalysis.

options.text: Nickel attracted to an external magnetic field
options.why: Unpaired electrons in nickel's incomplete 3d sublevel align with an external field, giving rise to paramagnetism.

options.text: High melting points of first-row transition metals
options.why: High melting points arise from strong metallic bonding involving both s and d electrons, not specifically from the d-sublevel being incomplete.

options.text: L- and D-lactic acid in muscle vs bacterial fermentation
options.why: Both share formula CH₃CH(OH)COOH but L-lactic acid forms in muscle during exercise and D-lactic acid in bacterial fermentation.

options.text: Thalidomide enantiomers with opposite physiological effects
options.why: One enantiomer was a sedative; the other caused birth defects. Same formula, entirely different biological activity at the chiral center.

options.text: Cis- and trans-but-2-ene having different boiling points
options.why: Cis and trans isomers differ in properties, but they arise from restricted rotation around C=C, not from a chiral carbon atom.

options.text: L- and D-amino acids recognized differently by enzymes
options.why: Only L-amino acids are incorporated into proteins; D-amino acids have identical formulas but are not recognized by the same biosynthetic enzymes.

options.text: R- and S-carvone having different characteristic odors
options.why: R-carvone smells of spearmint and S-carvone of caraway; same formula C₁₀H₁₄O but entirely different properties in chiral receptor environments.

options.text: Butan-1-ol and diethyl ether both with formula C₄H₁₀O
options.why: These two C₄H₁₀O compounds differ in connectivity rather than in the arrangement around a chiral center; they are structural isomers, not enantiomers.

options.text: Ethanol C₂H₆O vs dimethyl ether C₂H₆O
options.why: Ethanol has a hydroxy group and dimethyl ether has an alkoxy linkage; same formula but completely different functional class and reactivity.

options.text: Propanal C₃H₆O vs propanone C₃H₆O
options.why: Propanal is an aldehyde with a terminal carbonyl while propanone is a ketone; same formula C₃H₆O but different functional groups.

options.text: But-1-ene and but-2-ene both formula C₄H₈
options.why: Both are alkenes differing only in double bond position; they share the same functional class and are positional isomers, not functional group isomers.

options.text: Glucose C₆H₁₂O₆ aldose vs fructose C₆H₁₂O₆ ketose
options.why: Glucose has an aldehyde group and fructose a ketone group; same molecular formula but different functional group classes.

options.text: Ethanoic acid C₂H₄O₂ vs methyl methanoate C₂H₄O₂
options.why: Ethanoic acid is a carboxylic acid and methyl methanoate is an ester; same formula C₂H₄O₂ but entirely different functional groups.

options.text: Hexane C₆H₁₄ and 2-methylpentane C₆H₁₄
options.why: Both are alkanes sharing formula C₆H₁₄, differing only in branching; they are chain isomers within the same functional class.

options.text: C=C double bond in hex-1-ene IR and reactivity
options.why: Pi bond in hex-1-ene enables electrophilic addition and gives a C=C stretch in the IR spectrum, both absent in the saturated analogue.

options.text: Benzene ring UV absorption and electrophilic substitution
options.why: Aromatic pi electrons absorb UV and undergo electrophilic substitution, both properties arising directly from the ring's unsaturation.

options.text: Hydroxy group -OH in propan-1-ol reactivity and IR
options.why: The OH group gives reactivity toward oxidizing agents and a broad IR absorption, but this arises from a polar functional group, not from unsaturation.

options.text: C=O in pentan-3-one IR fingerprint and nucleophilic addition
options.why: Pi bond in the ketone C=O produces a strong IR absorption near 1715 cm-1 and makes the carbonyl carbon electrophilic toward nucleophiles.

options.text: Triple bond in but-1-yne IR and enhanced reactivity
options.why: Two pi bonds in the alkyne triple bond make but-1-yne more reactive than an alkene and give a characteristic weak C-C stretch in the IR.

options.text: Chain length difference between pentane and octane in mass spectra
options.why: Octane has a higher molecular ion m/z than pentane, but this reflects greater molecular size, not any difference in unsaturation.

options.text: Haber process temperature compromise for rate and yield
options.why: Raising temperature speeds both reactions but shifts the exothermic equilibrium toward reactants, forcing a compromise between rate and yield.

options.text: Contact process SO₂ + O₂ equilibrium run at 450 C
options.why: Higher temperature increases rate but disfavors the exothermic forward step; 450 C balances an acceptable rate with a useful yield.

options.text: Sugar dissolving faster in hot water
options.why: Warming speeds dissolution, but dissolving a solid is a physical process, not a reversible chemical equilibrium with a temperature-dependent K.

options.text: N₂O₄ and NO₂ equilibrium in a sealed tube
options.why: Increasing temperature speeds both forward and reverse reactions and shifts the endothermic direction toward the brown NO₂ gas.

options.text: Esterification equilibrium heated under reflux
options.why: Heating speeds the reaction and shifts the slightly endothermic esterification equilibrium toward the ester product.

options.text: SN₂ substitution of 1-bromobutane with NaOH at higher temperature
options.why: Higher temperature speeds this substitution via the Arrhenius equation, but an irreversible SN₂ reaction has no equilibrium position to shift.

options.text: Combustion of methane to CO₂ and H₂O
options.why: C-H and O=O bonds in the reactants are collectively weaker than the C=O and O-H bonds formed in the products, releasing energy.

options.text: Formation of HF from H₂ and F₂
options.why: H-F is among the strongest bonds in chemistry; forming it from weaker H-H and F-F bonds releases substantial energy.

options.text: Dissolving NaCl in water at room temperature
options.why: Dissolving NaCl balances lattice enthalpy against hydration enthalpy; the energy change depends on competing ionic terms, not on molecular bond strengths.

options.text: Reduction of iron oxide by carbon monoxide
options.why: CO₂ product bonds are collectively stronger than those in Fe₂O₃ and CO reactants, providing the thermodynamic driving force for iron extraction.

options.text: Formation of water from hydrogen and oxygen
options.why: Two strong O-H bonds formed per water molecule replace one H-H and half an O=O; products are more strongly bonded overall.

options.text: Thermal decomposition of calcium carbonate
options.why: CaCO₃ decomposition is endothermic because bonds in the CaO and CO₂ products are collectively weaker than in the reactant carbonate.

options.text: Adding more N₂ to the Haber equilibrium mixture
options.why: Increasing [N₂] disturbs equilibrium; the system shifts right to consume the extra N₂ and restore balance.

options.text: Decreasing pressure on NO₂ and N₂O₄ equilibrium
options.why: Lower pressure favors the side with more moles of gas, shifting the equilibrium toward 2NO₂ when pressure drops.

options.text: Adding a catalyst to the Haber process equilibrium
options.why: A catalyst speeds both forward and reverse reactions by equal factors and does not shift the equilibrium position or change K.

options.text: Cooling an exothermic equilibrium reaction
options.why: Lowering temperature shifts equilibrium in the exothermic direction, partly replacing the heat removed.

options.text: Adding acid to a chromate-dichromate equilibrium
options.why: Increased [H+] shifts the 2CrO₄₂- equilibrium toward dichromate Cr₂O₇₂-, as Le Chatelier's principle predicts.

options.text: Changing pressure on H₂ + I₂ equilibrium with equal moles of gas
options.why: For H₂ + I₂ equilibrium 2HI, equal moles of gas on both sides means pressure changes cause no equilibrium shift; Le Chatelier predicts no movement.

options.text: Water as acid with ammonia and as base with HCl
options.why: Water donates a proton to ammonia and accepts one from HCl, making it genuinely amphoteric in Bronsted-Lowry terms.

options.text: Hydrogencarbonate ion HCO₃-
options.why: HCO₃- donates a proton to form CO₃₂- in strong base or accepts one to form H₂CO₃ in strong acid, acting as both acid and base.

options.text: Hydrochloric acid HCl in aqueous solution
options.why: HCl is a strong proton donor but has essentially no tendency to accept a proton in water, so it is acid-only, not amphoteric.

options.text: Amino acids in aqueous solution
options.why: NH₃+ can donate a proton and COO- can accept one in the same molecule, making amino acids genuinely amphoteric zwitterions.

options.text: Dihydrogen phosphate ion H₂PO₄-
options.why: H₂PO₄- can lose a proton to form HPO₄₂- or gain one to form H₃PO₄, acting as both a Bronsted-Lowry acid and base.

options.text: Ethanoate ion CH₃COO- in water
options.why: Ethanoate can accept a proton to form ethanoic acid but has no tendency to donate one to water, making it base-only, not amphoteric.

options.text: HI stronger acid than HF despite lower electronegativity
options.why: H-I is much weaker and longer than H-F; easier bond breaking makes HI a stronger acid despite iodine's lower electronegativity.

options.text: Trichloroacetic acid stronger than ethanoic acid
options.why: Three electronegative Cl atoms withdraw electron density from -COOH, stabilizing the carboxylate anion and increasing acid strength.

options.text: Concentrated HCl having lower pH than dilute HCl
options.why: More concentrated HCl gives a lower pH, but pH differences reflect concentration, not the intrinsic structural strength of the acid.

options.text: Phenol weaker acid than ethanoic acid
options.why: Phenoxide has less resonance stabilization than carboxylate, where charge is shared equally over two oxygens, making phenol weaker.

options.text: Methylamine slightly stronger base than ammonia
options.why: An electron-donating methyl group increases electron density on nitrogen, making the lone pair more available for proton acceptance.

options.text: Ethanoic acid reacting instantly with NaOH in solution
options.why: Rapid neutralization reflects the fast rate of proton transfer between ions in solution, not a structural measure of acid strength.

options.text: Iron in the Haber process regenerated unchanged
options.why: Iron provides a surface for N₂ and H₂ to adsorb and react, then is regenerated unchanged at the end of each catalytic cycle.

options.text: Enzymes releasing unchanged after each reaction
options.why: Enzymes form temporary enzyme-substrate complexes, lower activation energy, and are released intact to catalyze many further cycles.

options.text: NaOH speeding up ester hydrolysis
options.why: Sodium hydroxide is consumed in saponification to form a carboxylate salt; it is a reactant, not a catalyst that is regenerated.

options.text: Platinum in a catalytic converter
options.why: Platinum adsorbs CO and NOx, facilitates their reaction, and is regenerated intact, functioning as a heterogeneous catalyst.

options.text: Nickel catalyst in hydrogenation of vegetable oils
options.why: Nickel enables addition of H₂ across double bonds at accessible conditions, is filtered out, and can be reused without consumption.

options.text: Bromine in free radical chain reactions of alkanes
options.why: Bromine initiates the chain and Br• regenerates in propagation, but termination steps permanently consume Br•, so it is not truly regenerated overall.

options.text: Iodoalkanes reacting faster than chloroalkanes in SN₂
options.why: C-I bond is weaker and longer than C-Cl; it breaks more readily in the SN₂ transition state, giving iodoalkanes a faster substitution rate.

options.text: F₂ reacting more vigorously with alkanes than Cl₂
options.why: F-F bond enthalpy (158 kJ/mol) is much lower than Cl-Cl; easier homolytic fission allows radical initiation far more readily.

options.text: Higher temperature increasing the rate of a bromination
options.why: Warming increases rate by providing more molecules with energy above Ea; the C-H bond strength itself is unchanged by temperature.

options.text: H-F bond strength making HF weaker acid than HCl
options.why: Strong, short H-F bond requires more energy to break than H-Cl, making proton donation from HF more difficult and reducing its acid strength.

options.text: Weak O-O single bond in ozone giving higher reactivity than O₂
options.why: Ozone's weaker O-O bond releases an oxygen atom far more readily than O₂'s strong double bond, giving O₃ much higher reactivity.

options.text: Larger molecules having fewer collisions per second at the same temperature
options.why: Larger molecules move slower at a given temperature, reducing collision frequency through a physical effect unrelated to bond strength.

options.text: Zinc-copper Daniell cell generating a current
options.why: Spontaneous zinc oxidation and copper ion reduction drives electron flow through the external circuit, converting chemical to electrical energy.

options.text: Hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell producing electricity
options.why: Hydrogen and oxygen react spontaneously at the electrodes, releasing energy as electrical current with water as the only product.

options.text: Electrolysis of dilute sulfuric acid
options.why: Electrolysis requires an external DC supply to drive non-spontaneous water decomposition; it converts electrical energy to chemical energy.

options.text: Lithium-ion battery discharging in a device
options.why: Spontaneous lithium oxidation and cathode reduction during discharge drive current through the external circuit.

options.text: Copper half-cell coupled with standard hydrogen electrode
options.why: Copper ions spontaneously oxidize hydrogen gas, giving a positive cell potential and a usable current in the external circuit.

options.text: Charging a nickel-metal hydride rechargeable battery
options.why: Recharging uses external electrical energy to reverse the spontaneous discharge reactions; charging converts electrical to chemical energy.

options.text: SN₁ hydrolysis of a tertiary halogenoalkane
options.why: Rate depends only on [halogenoalkane] because the slow unimolecular ionization step does not involve the nucleophile.

options.text: Iodination of propanone zero order in iodine
options.why: Rate is independent of [I₂] because iodine reacts only after the slow enolization step, which does not involve iodine at all.

options.text: Overall stoichiometry of a reaction predicting its rate orders
options.why: Rate laws cannot be deduced from overall equations; only the rate-determining elementary step sets the rate orders.

options.text: SN₂ reaction of 1-bromobutane with NaOH
options.why: Rate depends on both [halogenoalkane] and [NaOH] because the single concerted step involves both molecules in the transition state.

options.text: Elementary step molecularity in multi-step ozone decomposition
options.why: Each elementary step contributes its participants to the rate expression; the overall rate law reflects the identity of the rate-determining step.

options.text: Activation energy determining the form of the rate equation
options.why: Lower Ea increases k but does not set the algebraic form of the rate equation; only the mechanism determines the rate orders.

options.text: Ammonia NH₃ attacking halogenoalkanes and forming complex ions
options.why: NH₃ attacks halogenoalkanes via its lone pair in organic substitution and donates the same lone pair to metal cations in inorganic complex formation.

options.text: Water attacking acyl chlorides and coordinating to metal ions
options.why: Water hydrolyzes acyl chlorides by lone-pair donation in organic chemistry and acts as a ligand toward metal cations in inorganic complexes.

options.text: Bromine Br₂ decolorized by alkene pi bonds
options.why: Bromine accepts electron density from the alkene pi bond, acting as an electrophile, not a nucleophile.

options.text: Cyanide ion CN- in SN₂ reactions and as inorganic ligand
options.why: CN- attacks primary halogenoalkanes by lone-pair donation and coordinates to metal cations to form complex ions in inorganic chemistry.

options.text: Chloride ion Cl- as nucleophile and as ligand to metals
options.why: Cl- displaces leaving groups in organic SN₂ reactions and donates lone pairs to form coordinate bonds with transition metal cations.

options.text: Boron trifluoride BF₃ in Lewis acid-base reactions
options.why: BF₃ accepts electron pairs from nucleophiles; it is a Lewis acid (electrophile), not a nucleophile.

options.text: Free radical chlorination of ethane
options.why: Cl• radicals are highly reactive and abstract any C-H bond without preference, producing multiple mono- and poly-chlorinated products.

options.text: Radical polymerization of propene giving broad chain length distribution
options.why: Propagating radicals react in various orientations and terminate randomly, giving a statistical distribution of chain lengths rather than one product.

options.text: Electrophilic addition of HBr to propene
options.why: Markovnikov's rule directs HBr predominantly to one product by favoring the more stable carbocation; selectivity operates here.

options.text: Free radical bromination of butane giving two products
options.why: Br• can abstract any of the ten C-H hydrogens in butane, giving 1-bromobutane and 2-bromobutane in a ratio set by statistics.

options.text: Random termination in radical chain polymerization
options.why: Random coupling of propagating radical ends at termination produces a statistical spread of molecular weights, not a single defined product.

options.text: Acid-catalyzed esterification of ethanol with ethanoic acid
options.why: Esterification gives one defined ester product; the equilibrium mixture contains reactants and products but not through a non-selective intermediate.

options.text: Ammonium nitrate dissolving endothermically in water
options.why: NH₄NO₃ dissolves endothermically yet spontaneously because the large positional entropy of dispersed ions overcomes the unfavorable enthalpy.

options.text: Thermal decomposition of CaCO₃ above 840 C
options.why: CaCO₃ decomposition is endothermic, but above the critical temperature the TdeltaS term from gaseous CO₂ produced makes deltaG negative.

options.text: Combustion of ethanol in excess air
options.why: Combustion is overwhelmingly spontaneous because it is strongly exothermic; any entropy increase is a secondary factor, not the main driver here.

options.text: Liquid bromine vaporizing above its boiling point
options.why: Evaporation requires energy, but above the boiling point the large entropy increase of the gaseous state makes the process spontaneous.

options.text: Two ideal gases mixing spontaneously
options.why: Mixing two ideal gases involves no enthalpy change at all; the process is driven entirely by the entropy increase of the mixed state.

options.text: Water freezing spontaneously at -10 C
options.why: Freezing is spontaneous here because crystallization is exothermic; entropy decreases as the solid forms, so enthalpy drives the process.

options.text: BF₃ accepting lone pair from NH₃ forming BF₃ dot NH₃
options.why: BF₃ is a Lewis acid accepting the lone pair of the Lewis base NH₃; the adduct forms without any proton transfer.

options.text: Al₃+ coordinating water molecules in aqueous solution
options.why: Al₃+ accepts lone pairs from water, acting as a Lewis acid; this explains the acidity of Al₃+ solutions without invoking proton transfer.

options.text: NaOH neutralizing HCl in aqueous solution
options.why: Strong acid-base neutralization is completely described by proton transfer; Lewis acid-base framing adds no additional insight here.

options.text: Fe₃+ forming blood-red complex with thiocyanate SCN-
options.why: Fe₃+ accepts the lone pair from SCN-, acting as a Lewis acid in a reaction better described as Lewis acid-base than proton transfer.

options.text: SO₃ reacting with water to give sulfuric acid
options.why: SO₃ accepts a lone pair from the oxygen of water, acting as a Lewis acid; formation of H₂SO₄ is driven by this Lewis acid-base interaction.

options.text: AgCl precipitating when NaCl is added to AgNO₃ solution
options.why: Ag+ attracting Cl- drives precipitation through lattice energy; describing it as Lewis acid-base adds nothing useful to understanding why AgCl precipitates.

options.text: Zinc displacing copper from copper sulfate solution
options.why: Zn has more negative E° than Cu, giving a positive E°cell; a positive cell potential confirms thermodynamic spontaneity.

options.text: Chlorine oxidizing bromide ions in solution
options.why: Cl₂ has more positive E° than Br₂; coupling Cl₂ reduction with Br- oxidation gives a positive E°cell, predicting spontaneous reaction.

options.text: Sodium reacting violently and rapidly with water
options.why: Sodium reacts spontaneously with water, but E° data predicts thermodynamic spontaneity, not the vigor or rate of the reaction.

options.text: Copper not dissolving in dilute sulfuric acid
options.why: Copper has more positive E° than H+/H₂, giving a negative E°cell for the reaction with H₂SO₄; E° data correctly predicts no spontaneous reaction.

options.text: Iron rusting spontaneously in aerated water
options.why: Iron has more negative E° than O₂/H₂O, giving a positive E°cell; standard electrode potentials confirm that rusting is thermodynamically spontaneous.

options.text: Aluminum resisting corrosion despite very negative E°
options.why: Aluminum's negative E° predicts high reactivity, but a protective oxide film prevents reaction; kinetics override the thermodynamic prediction.

options.text: Oxidation of ethanol to ethanal with acidified dichromate
options.why: Carbon of CH₂OH increases in oxidation state as it loses H and gains bonds to O, converting primary alcohol to aldehyde.

options.text: Reduction of propanoic acid to propanal with LiAlH₄
options.why: Each hydride delivered to the carbonyl carbon decreases its oxidation state, converting the carboxylic acid stepwise toward alcohol.

options.text: Hydrolysis of ethyl ethanoate to ethanoic acid and ethanol
options.why: In ester hydrolysis the C-O bonds break and re-form, but the oxidation state of each carbon atom remains unchanged throughout.

options.text: Combustion of propane fully oxidizing carbon to CO₂
options.why: Carbon in propane (approximately -2 per CH₂) is fully oxidized to +4 in CO₂; combustion represents an extreme increase in carbon oxidation state.

options.text: Hydrogenation of ethene to ethane
options.why: Adding H₂ across the C=C double bond reduces each carbon from roughly -1 to -2 in ethane, lowering the carbon oxidation state.

options.text: Esterification of methanol with methanoic acid
options.why: Esterification forms a C-O-C linkage by loss of water; the oxidation states of carbon atoms in the ester match those in the original reactants.

options.text: 1-iodobutane reacting faster than 1-chlorobutane with NaOH
options.why: Iodide is a better leaving group than chloride because C-I is weaker and longer, breaking more easily in the SN₂ transition state.

options.text: Tosylate esters hydrolyzing much faster than free alcohols
options.why: Tosylate replaces the poor leaving group OH- with a stable, well-solvated anion, greatly accelerating substitution.

options.text: Tertiary halogenoalkane faster than primary in SN₁
options.why: Faster rate for tertiary substrates reflects carbocation stabilization by alkyl groups, not any difference in the leaving group identity.

options.text: Fluoroalkanes almost unreactive under standard SN₂ conditions
options.why: Fluoride is the weakest leaving group among halogens because C-F is the strongest bond, making departure nearly impossible.

options.text: Mesylate esters as highly reactive substrates in substitution
options.why: Mesylate departs as a stable resonance-stabilized anion, making it one of the most effective leaving groups and greatly enhancing rate.

options.text: Halving nucleophile concentration reducing SN₂ rate by half
options.why: Reducing [NaOH] cuts SN₂ rate because rate = k[substrate][nucleophile]; this is a nucleophile concentration effect, not a leaving group effect.

options.text: Insufficient oxygen in car engine producing CO not CO₂
options.why: When oxygen is limited, carbon is oxidized only to toxic CO instead of CO₂, directly creating a health hazard from the changed product.

options.text: Limited air in coal combustion producing soot and CO
options.why: Restricting air converts combustion products from CO₂ and water to particulate soot and CO, harming both air quality and human health.

options.text: Adding excess HCl beyond equivalence point in a titration
options.why: Excess HCl leaves acid in solution but does not change the salt product formed, and there are no health or environmental consequences.

options.text: Controlled O₂ supply in Ostwald process giving NO not N₂
options.why: Controlling the O₂ supply determines whether ammonia is oxidized to useful NO or wasted as N₂, directly affecting the industrial process.

options.text: Oxygen-limited soil producing N₂O greenhouse gas not N₂
options.why: Under anaerobic conditions, bacteria produce potent greenhouse gas N₂O rather than harmless N₂, linking oxygen limitation to climate impact.

options.text: Finding the limiting reactant to calculate theoretical yield
options.why: Identifying the limiting reactant enables a yield calculation but is a stoichiometric exercise with no change in products or environmental effect.

options.text: Sodium reacting with water to give NaOH and H₂
options.why: Sodium is oxidized from 0 to +1 while H+ from water is reduced to H₂; water simultaneously donates protons as a Bronsted-Lowry acid.

options.text: Zinc dissolving in dilute hydrochloric acid
options.why: Zinc is oxidized to Zn₂+ while H+ is reduced to H₂; the acid acts simultaneously as a proton donor and as an oxidizing agent.

options.text: Neutralization of NaOH with nitric acid HNO₃
options.why: H+ transfers to OH- in a straightforward proton-transfer reaction; no oxidation state changes occur and there is no redox component.

options.text: Fluorine reacting with water to give HF and O₂
options.why: Fluorine oxidizes water oxygen from -2 to 0 while being reduced from 0 to -1; HF is also formed as an acidic product simultaneously.

options.text: Chlorine disproportionating in cold sodium hydroxide
options.why: Cl₂ simultaneously oxidizes one chlorine to +1 and reduces another to -1; the product NaOCl is the salt of a weak acid, adding acid-base character.

options.text: Barium sulfate precipitating from BaCl₂ and dilute H₂SO₄
options.why: Ba₂+ and SO₄₂- form an insoluble precipitate; H₂SO₄ donates protons as an acid, but no oxidation state changes occur in this precipitation.
